items.q: Why does the colour of a transition metal complex change when ligands are exchanged?
items.correct: Different ligands cause different amounts of d-orbital splitting, so different wavelengths of light are absorbed.
items.misconception: The colour changes because the metal ion itself changes oxidation state.
items.random_wrong: The colour changes because the new ligand is itself coloured.
items.why: Students assume colour comes from the ligand or from a redox change; they miss that the same metal ion gives different colours depending on which ligands are bound.

items.q: If 50 cm^3 of a 1 mol/dm^3 solution is diluted to 100 cm^3, what is the new concentration?
items.correct: 0.5 mol/dm^3 — the moles stay the same but the volume doubled.
items.misconception: The new concentration is 0.05 mol/dm^3 because you doubled the volume so you halve the concentration once and shift the decimal too.
items.random_wrong: The new concentration is 2 mol/dm^3 because adding water makes the solution stronger.
items.why: Students panic with unit conversions and over-divide, or they confuse 'add water = more concentrated' through a misread of the procedure.

items.q: Why does first ionisation energy generally increase across a period?
items.correct: Nuclear charge increases while shielding stays roughly constant, so electrons are held more tightly.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger across a period, making electrons easier to add but harder to remove.
items.random_wrong: Atoms across a period have more shells, pulling electrons closer.
items.why: Students confuse the direction of the atomic-radius trend across a period (it actually decreases) and use wrong size reasoning.

items.q: At half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration:
items.why: Correct answer: pH = pKa. “pH = 7” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Phenolphthalein. “Methyl orange” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A buffer with [A⁻]/[HA] = 10 and pKa = 4.5 has pH:
items.why: The real figure is 5.5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
